name: Euclid
summary: Founder of systematic geometry who compiled the Elements, the most influential mathematical textbook ever written.
remarkableFact: The Elements remained the primary geometry textbook for over 2000 years, used continuously until the 20th century.

name: Pythagoras
summary: Ancient mathematician and philosopher whose famous theorem about right triangles became foundational to mathematics and physics.
remarkableFact: Pythagoras believed numbers were the fundamental reality of the universe and that music, mathematics, and the cosmos were intimately connected.

name: Archimedes
summary: Brilliant ancient mathematician and engineer who invented the method of exhaustion and made breakthrough discoveries in mechanics and hydrostatics.
remarkableFact: Archimedes reportedly ran naked through the streets shouting Eureka! after discovering the principle of buoyancy while bathing.

name: Eratosthenes
summary: Ancient mathematician and geographer who invented the sieve algorithm for finding prime numbers and accurately calculated Earth's circumference.
remarkableFact: Eratosthenes calculated Earth's circumference to within 2% of the actual value using only geometry and observations of the sun's shadow.

name: Apollonius
summary: Ancient mathematician whose treatise on conic sections was the most comprehensive treatment until the Renaissance.
remarkableFact: Apollonius introduced the terms ellipse, parabola, and hyperbola for the three types of conic sections that are still used today.

name: Diophantus
summary: Ancient Greek mathematician whose work on algebraic equations and integer solutions became foundational to number theory.
remarkableFact: Diophantine equations, named after him, remain central to modern mathematics, with their solutions still puzzling mathematicians today.

name: Muhammad al-Khwarizmi
summary: Persian mathematician who wrote the foundational algebra textbook, from whose name the word algorithm derives.
remarkableFact: The word algorithm comes from the Latinization of al-Khwarizmi's name, forever linking his identity to computational procedures.

name: Leonardo Fibonacci
summary: Medieval mathematician who introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to Europe and discovered the famous sequence now bearing his name.
remarkableFact: The Fibonacci sequence appears throughout nature in flower petals, spiral shells, and galaxy formations, making it one of mathematics' greatest discoveries.

name: Gerolamo Cardano
summary: Renaissance mathematician who published the first systematic treatment of cubic and quartic equations, and wrote early work on probability.
remarkableFact: Cardano was a physician, philosopher, and gambler whose mathematical insights into games of chance laid groundwork for probability theory.

name: Niccolò Tartaglia
summary: Renaissance mathematician who solved the general cubic equation and contributed to ballistics and engineering mathematics.
remarkableFact: Tartaglia was largely self-taught, acquiring his mathematical knowledge without formal university education despite early trauma from a sword wound.

name: Isaac Newton
summary: Revolutionary mathematician and physicist who invented calculus, formulated laws of motion and gravitation, and transformed science.
remarkableFact: Newton did his most groundbreaking work during the Great Plague when Cambridge University closed, spending a year inventing calculus and discovering gravity.

name: Gottfried Leibniz
summary: Polymath mathematician and philosopher who independently invented calculus and made fundamental contributions to logic and symbolic notation.
remarkableFact: Leibniz's notation for calculus (dy/dx, integral signs) proved so superior that it replaced Newton's, becoming standard in mathematics worldwide.

name: Leonhard Euler
summary: The most prolific mathematician in history, whose work touched virtually every branch of mathematics and physics.
remarkableFact: Euler continued producing mathematics even after going blind, dictating to a scribe and averaging 800 pages of published work per year.

name: Jacob Bernoulli
summary: Pioneer of probability theory and calculus whose Law of Large Numbers became fundamental to statistics.
remarkableFact: Jacob requested that a logarithmic spiral be carved on his tombstone with the inscription 'I shall arise the same, though changed.'

name: Daniel Bernoulli
summary: Son of Johann Bernoulli who made major contributions to probability, fluid mechanics, and the theory of risk.
remarkableFact: Daniel Bernoulli solved the St. Petersburg paradox, demonstrating that expected utility differs from expected value in economics.

name: Pierre-Simon Laplace
summary: Mathematician and astronomer whose work unified probability theory, statistics, and celestial mechanics.
remarkableFact: Laplace famously claimed that if we knew the position and momentum of all particles, we could predict the entire future, spawning determinism debates.

name: Joseph-Louis Lagrange
summary: Versatile mathematician who made fundamental contributions to calculus, mechanics, algebra, and number theory.
remarkableFact: Lagrange developed analytic mechanics, reformulating Newton's laws using calculus in a way that unified mechanics and provided powerful problem-solving techniques.

name: Carl Friedrich Gauss
summary: Prince of mathematicians whose contributions span number theory, statistics, geometry, and physics with unmatched depth.
remarkableFact: As a child, Gauss instantly summed numbers 1 to 100 by recognizing a pattern, and his career maintained this level of genius.

name: Joseph Fourier
summary: Mathematician and physicist who developed Fourier analysis and series to solve heat conduction problems, revolutionizing signal processing.
remarkableFact: Fourier analysis is ubiquitous in modern technology, from JPEG compression to WiFi, yet he developed it to understand heat diffusion.

name: Thomas Bayes
summary: Presbyterian minister whose essay on probability introduced Bayes' theorem, fundamental to Bayesian inference and machine learning.
remarkableFact: Bayes' theorem was published posthumously and largely ignored until the 20th century, when it became essential to modern statistics and AI.

name: Augustin-Louis Cauchy
summary: Prolific mathematician who formalized mathematical analysis, rigorous foundations for calculus, and complex analysis.
remarkableFact: Cauchy was extraordinarily prolific, publishing nearly 800 papers and working in nearly every area of mathematics with rigorous proofs.

name: Niels Henrik Abel
summary: Brilliant but short-lived mathematician who proved the unsolvability of quintic equations and contributed to elliptic functions.
remarkableFact: Abel died of tuberculosis at 26, yet left such profound work that the Abel Prize, mathematics' highest honor, is named after him.

name: Évariste Galois
summary: Revolutionary mathematician who developed group theory and proved the unsolvability of polynomial equations by radicals.
remarkableFact: Galois died in a duel at 20, supposedly over a woman, leaving behind mathematical work so revolutionary it took decades to understand.

name: Bernhard Riemann
summary: Profound mathematician whose ideas on geometry, analysis, and number theory shaped modern mathematics and Einstein's relativity.
remarkableFact: The Riemann Hypothesis, one of seven Millennium Prize Problems, remains unsolved and worth one million dollars, despite Riemann's suggestive insights.

name: Georg Cantor
summary: Mathematician who created set theory and revealed the hierarchy of infinities, revolutionizing the foundations of mathematics.
remarkableFact: Cantor's radical ideas about infinity were initially rejected and ridiculed; he suffered mental illness partly from the mathematical establishment's criticism.

name: Richard Dedekind
summary: Mathematician who developed set-theoretic foundations for number systems and created the notion of algebraic integers.
remarkableFact: Dedekind's Cuts provided the first rigorous definition of real numbers, resolving centuries of uncertainty about the nature of continuous quantities.

name: Karl Weierstrass
summary: Rigorous mathematician who formalized real analysis, defined limits precisely, and discovered pathological functions challenging mathematical intuition.
remarkableFact: Weierstrass discovered a continuous function with no derivative anywhere, shocking mathematicians and showing that intuition can mislead.

name: Bernard Bolzano
summary: Mathematician and philosopher who anticipated modern rigor in analysis, contributed to logic, and studied infinite sets.
remarkableFact: Bolzano proved the Intermediate Value Theorem rigorously without using geometric intuition, exemplifying the revolution toward logical rigor.

name: George Boole
summary: Mathematician and logician who created Boolean algebra, the mathematical foundation of logic and digital computer design.
remarkableFact: Boolean algebra was largely ignored until the 20th century, when it became essential to digital electronics and computing.

name: Arthur Cayley
summary: Prolific mathematician who developed matrix algebra, advanced group theory, and contributed to non-Euclidean geometry.
remarkableFact: Cayley introduced matrices in 1858 and developed matrix algebra, yet matrices didn't become central to mathematics until the 20th century.

name: William Rowan Hamilton
summary: Brilliant mathematician who invented quaternions, revolutionized mechanics, and contributed to algebra and geometry.
remarkableFact: Hamilton spent years obsessed with quaternions and carved the fundamental formula into a bridge in Dublin in a moment of inspiration.

name: Nikolai Lobachevsky
summary: Mathematician who developed non-Euclidean geometry, a hyperbolic geometry challenging Euclid's parallel postulate.
remarkableFact: Lobachevsky's non-Euclidean geometry was initially rejected but later proven consistent, revolutionizing mathematics and enabling Einstein's relativity.

name: Henri Poincaré
summary: Universal mathematician who made transformative contributions to topology, dynamical systems, and special relativity.
remarkableFact: Poincaré pioneered chaos theory by discovering sensitive dependence on initial conditions in three-body gravitational systems.

name: Emmy Noether
summary: Revolutionary mathematician whose theorem connecting symmetries to conservation laws became central to theoretical physics.
remarkableFact: Hilbert called Noether the greatest woman mathematician and her symmetry theorem unified mathematics and physics across quantum and classical domains.

name: Felix Klein
summary: Mathematician who unified geometry through group theory, developed the Klein bottle, and advanced non-Euclidean geometry.
remarkableFact: The Klein bottle, a non-orientable surface with no boundary, was introduced by Klein and cannot exist in three dimensions without self-intersection.

name: David Hilbert
summary: Towering mathematician whose influential problems shaped 20th century mathematics and who championed formal logic and axiomatization.
remarkableFact: Hilbert posed 23 unsolved problems in 1900, driving mathematical research for decades; some remain unsolved with millions in prize money.

name: Giuseppe Peano
summary: Logician and mathematician who formalized arithmetic with axioms, contributed to symbolic logic, and studied space-filling curves.
remarkableFact: Peano's space-filling curve, a continuous curve that passes through every point in a square, was initially thought impossible.

name: Gottlob Frege
summary: Philosopher and logician who founded modern logic and set theory, creating rigorous foundations for mathematics.
remarkableFact: Frege discovered Russell's paradox just before publishing his opus, a devastating blow showing inconsistency in his logical system.

name: James Joseph Sylvester
summary: Brilliant algebraist who advanced matrix theory, invariant theory, and contributed to combinatorics and number theory.
remarkableFact: Sylvester was expelled from Trinity College Cambridge for refusing to take religious oaths, yet became one of mathematics' greatest figures.

name: Pafnuty Chebyshev
summary: Russian mathematician who advanced approximation theory, number theory, and probability through practical problem-solving.
remarkableFact: Chebyshev developed orthogonal polynomials to solve practical engineering problems, showing mathematics' power for applications.

name: Andrey Markov
summary: Mathematician who developed Markov chains and advanced probability theory through analysis of dependent events.
remarkableFact: Markov chains, invented by Markov, model everything from stock prices to DNA sequences and are foundational to machine learning.

name: Aleksandr Lyapunov
summary: Mathematician who developed stability theory for dynamical systems and proved the Central Limit Theorem under weak conditions.
remarkableFact: Lyapunov stability, essential for control theory and engineering, determines whether systems return to equilibrium after perturbation.

name: Kurt Gödel
summary: Logician who proved fundamental incompleteness theorems, showing mathematical truth exceeds what can be formally proven.
remarkableFact: Gödel's incompleteness theorems shattered Hilbert's dream of a complete axiomatic system, fundamentally limiting what mathematics can prove.

name: Alan Turing
summary: Logician and pioneering computer scientist who defined computability and developed the concept of the Turing machine.
remarkableFact: Turing's contributions to the Enigma codebreaking effort saved countless lives; he founded computer science, then died tragically at 41.

name: John von Neumann
summary: Brilliant mathematician who contributed to logic, set theory, quantum mechanics, game theory, and founded computer science.
remarkableFact: Von Neumann designed the modern stored-program computer architecture that all computers follow today, earning him computing's highest honor.

name: Claude Shannon
summary: Mathematical genius who founded information theory, showing how information can be quantified, transmitted, and protected.
remarkableFact: Shannon's master's thesis applied Boolean algebra to electrical circuits, creating digital logic; he also juggled, rode a unicycle, and played chess.

name: Andrey Kolmogorov
summary: Prolific mathematician whose axiomatization of probability theory, dynamical systems, and complexity theory transformed mathematics.
remarkableFact: Kolmogorov's axiomatization provided the rigorous foundation for probability theory, enabling its explosion as a mathematical discipline.

name: Srinivasa Ramanujan
summary: Largely self-taught Indian genius whose intuitive discoveries in number theory and analysis anticipated modern mathematics.
remarkableFact: Ramanujan made thousands of discoveries with minimal training; mentors like Hardy called him the greatest mathematician of his era.

name: Godfrey Harold Hardy
summary: Influential mathematician whose work on number theory and analysis advanced pure mathematics and mentored Ramanujan.
remarkableFact: Hardy wrote that mathematics is beautiful only when pursued without practical application, exemplifying pure mathematics philosophy.

name: Paul Erdős
summary: Prolific mathematician known for surprising results in discrete mathematics, graph theory, and number theory.
remarkableFact: Erdős lived nomadically, traveling constantly to collaborate and publishing over 1500 papers; mathematicians measure closeness by Erdős number.

name: Benoit Mandelbrot
summary: Mathematician who discovered fractals and the Mandelbrot set, revolutionizing understanding of self-similarity in nature.
remarkableFact: The Mandelbrot set, defined by a simple iteration, exhibits infinite complexity and is one of mathematics' most beautiful objects.

name: John Forbes Nash Jr.
summary: Mathematician who pioneered game theory and proved fundamental results about equilibrium, earning the Nobel Prize in Economics.
remarkableFact: Nash equilibrium, central to economics and evolution, describes situations where no player benefits from changing strategy unilaterally.

name: Norbert Wiener
summary: Mathematician who founded cybernetics and control theory, analyzing feedback systems in machines and living organisms.
remarkableFact: Wiener founded cybernetics, unifying control systems across machines and biological organisms through mathematics.

name: Harry Nyquist
summary: Engineer and mathematician who developed stability criteria and sampling theory fundamental to signal processing and control.
remarkableFact: The Nyquist frequency determines the minimum sampling rate needed to accurately represent signals, enabling digital audio and data.

name: Hendrik Bode
summary: Engineer and mathematician who developed Bode plots and contributed to control theory and network analysis.
remarkableFact: Bode plots, named after Bode, provide intuitive visualization of frequency response and are ubiquitous in engineering control systems.

name: Paul Dirac
summary: Theoretical physicist and mathematician who unified quantum mechanics and special relativity, predicting antimatter.
remarkableFact: Dirac's equation predicted the positron before it was discovered experimentally, one of physics' greatest triumphs.

name: Werner Heisenberg
summary: Physicist who formulated matrix mechanics and discovered the uncertainty principle, a cornerstone of quantum mechanics.
remarkableFact: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle shows that position and momentum cannot be simultaneously known with arbitrary precision.

name: Erwin Schrödinger
summary: Physicist who developed wave mechanics and the Schrödinger equation, describing quantum systems' evolution.
remarkableFact: Schrödinger's wave equation is the fundamental equation of quantum mechanics, describing how quantum systems evolve in time.

name: Ludwig Boltzmann
summary: Physicist who founded statistical mechanics, connecting microscopic molecular behavior to macroscopic thermodynamics.
remarkableFact: Boltzmann's constant and his tombstone equation S=k log W remain central to physics, explaining entropy and time's arrow.

name: James Clerk Maxwell
summary: Brilliant physicist who unified electricity and magnetism into Maxwell's equations, revolutionizing physics.
remarkableFact: Maxwell's equations showed that light is an electromagnetic wave, unifying optics with electromagnetism and predicting radio waves.

name: Albert Einstein
summary: Revolutionary physicist whose theories of relativity transformed our understanding of space, time, gravity, and energy.
remarkableFact: Einstein's E=mc² showed the equivalence of mass and energy, one of physics' most profound and practical insights.

name: Max Planck
summary: Physicist who founded quantum mechanics by introducing energy quanta, revolutionizing physics at small scales.
remarkableFact: Planck's constant determines the quantum-classical boundary and sets the scale of quantum effects throughout the universe.

name: Edward Lorenz
summary: Meteorologist and mathematician who discovered chaos theory and the butterfly effect in atmospheric modeling.
remarkableFact: Lorenz discovered that tiny differences in initial conditions lead to vastly different outcomes, explaining why weather is unpredictable.

name: Richard Feynman
summary: Brilliant physicist who revolutionized quantum electrodynamics and influenced physics with unorthodox visualization techniques.
remarkableFact: Feynman developed intuitive diagrams for quantum processes and promoted learning through deep understanding rather than rote memorization.

name: Fischer Black
summary: Mathematician and financial engineer who co-developed the Black-Scholes option pricing model, revolutionizing finance.
remarkableFact: The Black-Scholes formula transformed finance from art to science, enabling precise option pricing that became ubiquitous in markets.

name: Myron Scholes
summary: Mathematician who co-developed the Black-Scholes option pricing model with Fischer Black and Robert Merton.
remarkableFact: Scholes won the Nobel Prize in Economics for the Black-Scholes model, which became the standard for pricing derivatives.

name: Robert Merton
summary: Mathematician who extended the Black-Scholes model and developed stochastic calculus applications to finance.
remarkableFact: Merton extended Black-Scholes to account for multiple assets and founded Long-Term Capital Management, illustrating both theory's power and limits.

name: Andrew Wiles
summary: Mathematician who proved Fermat's Last Theorem in 1995, solving a problem unsolved for 358 years.
remarkableFact: Wiles spent seven years in secret proving Fermat's Last Theorem, finally solving mathematics' most famous unsolved problem.

name: Grigori Perelman
summary: Reclusive mathematician who proved the Poincaré Conjecture, one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems.
remarkableFact: Perelman declined the Fields Medal and the Millennium Prize of one million dollars, preferring solitude over recognition.

name: Terence Tao
summary: Prodigious mathematician whose work spans number theory, harmonic analysis, and combinatorics, winning the Fields Medal.
remarkableFact: Tao has made breakthrough contributions across multiple fields; he's known for his clear writing explaining advanced mathematics.

name: Yoshua Bengio
summary: Deep learning pioneer who contributed to backpropagation, convolutional networks, and attention mechanisms in artificial intelligence.
remarkableFact: Bengio won the Turing Award for contributions to deep learning, revolutionizing artificial intelligence and natural language processing.

name: Sepp Hochreiter
summary: Machine learning researcher who invented LSTM networks, enabling deep learning with long-term dependencies.
remarkableFact: Long Short-Term Memory networks invented by Hochreiter enabled breakthrough applications in speech recognition and language translation.

name: Larry Page
summary: Computer scientist who co-founded Google and developed PageRank, a mathematical algorithm that revolutionized web search.
remarkableFact: PageRank, based on treating the web as a Markov chain, became the foundation of Google's search dominance.

name: Sergey Brin
summary: Computer scientist who co-founded Google with Larry Page and developed PageRank algorithm with Page.
remarkableFact: Brin and Page's PageRank algorithm outcompeted existing search engines by modeling web authority through mathematical graph theory.

name: Edsger Dijkstra
summary: Computer scientist who developed Dijkstra's algorithm for finding shortest paths, fundamental to network routing.
remarkableFact: Dijkstra's algorithm efficiently finds the shortest path in networks and is used in GPS, routing, and countless applications.

name: Donald Knuth
summary: Computer scientist who wrote the definitive Art of Computer Programming and advanced analysis of algorithms.
remarkableFact: Knuth's Art of Computer Programming is considered the programmer's bible; he's known for being meticulous, even paying for errors found in his work.

name: William Vickrey
summary: Economist and mathematician who developed auction theory and mechanism design, revealing optimal bidding strategies.
remarkableFact: Vickrey auctions ensure truth-telling through clever mechanism design and are now used in online advertising platforms.

name: Roger Myerson
summary: Economist who developed mechanism design theory, showing how to optimally design games and auctions.
remarkableFact: Myerson won the Nobel Prize for mechanism design, which underpins modern auction platforms like Google Ads and eBay.

name: Satoshi Nakamoto
summary: Pseudonymous creator of Bitcoin who invented blockchain technology, revolutionizing distributed systems and cryptocurrency.
remarkableFact: Nakamoto's identity remains unknown despite Bitcoin's success; their anonymity paradoxically demonstrates their creation's decentralization.

name: Tim Berners-Lee
summary: Computer scientist who invented the World Wide Web, fundamentally changing how information is shared globally.
remarkableFact: Berners-Lee refused to patent the Web, allowing it to become free and open, arguably the most transformative decision in computing history.

name: Blaise Pascal
summary: Mathematician and philosopher who contributed to probability theory, geometry, and developed mechanical calculation devices.
remarkableFact: Pascal's triangle and Pascal's principle appear across mathematics and physics, yet he abandoned mathematics for theology in his final years.

name: Pierre de Fermat
summary: Mathematician and lawyer whose work in number theory, optics, and calculus influenced modern mathematics despite publishing little.
remarkableFact: Fermat's Last Theorem, a handwritten note in a book margin, remained unsolved for 358 years until Andrew Wiles proved it in 1995.

name: René Descartes
summary: Philosopher and mathematician who invented coordinate geometry, linking algebra and geometry in revolutionary ways.
remarkableFact: Descartes developed coordinate geometry while lying in bed during illness, revolutionizing mathematics through algebraic representation of geometry.

name: Gregory Newton
summary: Mathematician known for Newton's method and forward difference formulas, contributing to numerical analysis.
remarkableFact: Newton's method for finding roots converges quadratically, making it one of the most powerful and widely used numerical algorithms.

name: Brahmagupta
summary: Indian mathematician who introduced zero as a number and developed algebraic techniques in ancient mathematics.
remarkableFact: Brahmagupta's work on zero was revolutionary; he treated zero as a number, not just a placeholder, enabling modern arithmetic.

name: Bhaskara II
summary: Medieval Indian mathematician who solved quadratic equations, studied infinite series, and made astronomical calculations.
remarkableFact: Bhaskara understood zero and negative numbers intuitively, solving problems centuries before European mathematicians grasped these concepts.

name: August Möbius
summary: Mathematician and astronomer who discovered the Möbius strip and made contributions to projective geometry.
remarkableFact: The Möbius strip, with only one side and one edge, is a non-orientable surface that confounds intuition and revolutionized topology.

name: William Clifford
summary: Mathematician who developed geometric algebra and contributed to non-Euclidean geometry before his early death.
remarkableFact: Clifford developed geometric algebra at 34, combining quaternions and exterior algebra into a unified framework.

name: Geoffrey Hinton
summary: Cognitive scientist and machine learning pioneer who developed backpropagation and deep learning techniques.
remarkableFact: Hinton won the Turing Award for developing backpropagation, enabling the deep learning revolution that transformed AI.

name: Yann LeCun
summary: Computer scientist who invented convolutional neural networks, revolutionizing image recognition and deep learning.
remarkableFact: LeCun's convolutional networks enabled modern computer vision; they're used in facial recognition, autonomous vehicles, and medical imaging.

name: Ilya Sutskever
summary: Machine learning researcher who developed sequence-to-sequence models and led deep learning research at OpenAI.
remarkableFact: Sutskever's sequence-to-sequence models enabled neural machine translation, powering Google Translate and modern language models.
